title: The Eternal Decree of God, Part 4
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Eternal Decree of God_ Part 4 (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt
outline: Concluding the series on WCF Chapter 3
WCF 3.6 and 3.8: summaries of later chapters on salvation
Effectual calling, justification, adoption, sanctification, glorification
These are previews of doctrines covered in later WCF chapters
Conclusion: the eternal decree as the foundation of all of salvation
summary: Concluding lesson of the four-part series on the eternal decree, covering WCF Chapter 3 paragraphs 6 and 8. These paragraphs preview the golden chain of salvation: effectual calling, justification, adoption, sanctification, and glorification. Each of these is grounded in the eternal decree of God and will be expanded in later chapters of the Confession. The lesson closes out the January series on divine sovereignty.
transcript:
We have our last lesson on chapter 3 of the Westminster Confession of Faith, on God's eternal decree. We are going to consider two paragraphs and then be done with this chapter for a while. These two paragraphs are essentially a summary of several chapters that come later in the Confession. If you want to learn more about effectual calling, justification, adoption, sanctification, and glorification, there are chapters later in the Confession that address all of those things as well.

We are going to look at chapter 3, paragraphs 6 and 8. These are also in your hymnal on page 850. We will read through them and then walk through paragraph 6 clause by clause, and then look at paragraph 8.

## WCF Chapter 3, Paragraph 6

> *"As God hath appointed the Elect unto glory, so hath He, by the eternal and most free purpose of His will, foreordained all the means thereunto. Wherefore they who are elected, being fallen in Adam, are redeemed by Christ; are effectually called unto faith in Christ by His Spirit working in due season; are justified, adopted, sanctified, and kept by His power through faith unto salvation. Neither are any other redeemed by Christ, effectually called, justified, adopted, sanctified, and saved, but the Elect only."*

## WCF Chapter 3, Paragraph 8

> *"The doctrine of this high mystery of Predestination is to be handled with special prudence and care, that men attending the will of God revealed in His Word, and yielding obedience thereunto, may, from the certainty of their effectual vocation, be assured of their eternal election. So shall this doctrine afford matter of praise, reverence, and admiration of God; and of humility, diligence, and abundant consolation to all that sincerely obey the Gospel."*

Now let us work through paragraph 6 clause by clause.

## Clause 1: "As God hath appointed the Elect unto glory"

The goal of election is not merely that people be saved — it is that they go all the way to glory. We see this in Romans 8:29–30:

> *"For whom He foreknew, He also predestined to be conformed to the image of His Son, that He might be the firstborn among many brethren. Moreover whom He predestined, these He also called; whom He called, these He also justified; and whom He justified, these He also glorified."* (Romans 8:29–30)

The same group that was predestined is called, and the called are justified, and the justified are glorified. It is the same group from beginning to end. The goal of election is the glorification of the elect.

That has a very practical implication: we are not just called to share the gospel so that people can be "saved" and then we move on. We are to be instruments in God's hands in helping people get to glory — which means a lifelong relationship of growth, not just a one-time event.

Paul says in Romans 5:1–2:

> *"Therefore, having been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ, through whom also we have access by faith into this grace in which we stand, and rejoice in hope of the glory of God."* (Romans 5:1–2)

We rejoice in the hope of the glory of God — that is our final destination. The purpose of election is to restore the relationship between man, the creature, and God, the Creator, that was destroyed by the fall.

## Clause 2: "By the eternal and most free purpose of His will, foreordained all the means thereunto"

God not only appointed a group of people to get to glory — he also appointed how that is going to happen. Doctrinally this means that God chose to accomplish the glorification of his people by ordaining effectual calling, justification, adoption, sanctification, and ultimately glorification.

How do we get to glory? First, God changes our hearts so that we have a heart able to believe. Then we believe in the Lord Jesus Christ. We are adopted by the Father through faith. We are definitively sanctified — set apart as holy. And then we are progressively sanctified and, at the return of Christ, we become entirely like him, both body and soul.

When you understand this, books like Hebrews make much more sense. Five times in Hebrews the Holy Spirit says, in essence: encourage one another so that you do not fall away. God appointed the means — including the ministry of the people of God to one another — to help the elect get to the end.

There is another practical application here: there is only one way to come to God. Not only has God appointed that we will get to glory — he has also appointed the way in which we will get there. And that way is Jesus Christ. Jesus himself says in John 14:6:

> *"I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through Me."* (John 14:6)

Peter says in Acts 4:11–12:

> *"This is the 'stone which was rejected by you builders, which has become the chief cornerstone.' Nor is there salvation in any other, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved."* (Acts 4:11–12)

Paul says in 1 Timothy 2:5: "For there is one God and one Mediator between God and men, the Man Christ Jesus."

This doctrine therefore denies the possibility of being saved apart from the gospel of Jesus Christ. Different religions will not save you. If someone is not claiming the Jesus of the Bible to be their Savior, it will not get them to glory. Simply worshiping one God does not mean you are worshiping the same God. Judaism does not get to glory. Islam does not get to glory. The Christian cults — Mormonism, Jehovah's Witnesses, Christian Science — do not get to God, because they worship a different God and not through the means God established for arriving at glory.

This also means that places where the gospel has not arrived — those people are perishing, because they have not come to know Jesus. And the Scriptures place responsibility for that on the church. Romans 10 asks: why have they not called on the name of the Lord? Because no one has been sent to proclaim to them. Who does the sending? The church. That is where the responsibility lies.

## Clause 3: "They who are elected, being fallen in Adam, are redeemed by Christ"

Who are those who fell in Adam? All humanity. Adam was the representative head of the human race. Turn to Romans 5:12:

> *"Therefore, just as through one man sin entered the world, and death through sin, and thus death spread to all men, because all sinned..."* (Romans 5:12)

When someone asks, "What about the innocent person on a remote island who never heard of Jesus?" the answer is yes, they will be held accountable — but the problem is that no such innocent person exists. We all sinned in Adam. Even if you could keep the law of God perfectly from birth, you would still face the judgment because of Adam's sin.

Paul summarizes in 1 Corinthians 15:22: "For as in Adam all die, even so in Christ all shall be made alive."

Look at Romans 5:17–21:

> *"For if by the one man's offense death reigned through the one, much more those who receive abundance of grace and of the gift of righteousness will reign in life through the One, Jesus Christ. Therefore, as through one man's offense judgment came to all men, resulting in condemnation, even so through one Man's righteous act the free gift came to all men, resulting in justification of life. For as by one man's disobedience many were made sinners, so also by one Man's obedience many will be made righteous... so that as sin reigned in death, even so grace might reign through righteousness to eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord."* (Romans 5:17–21)

All those represented by Christ are redeemed by his sacrifice.

What does redemption mean? It means to buy back — the payment of a price for the deliverance of God's people. Redemption is not the same as what we usually mean by salvation. Redemption is accomplished by Christ at the cross and resurrection for all the elect — it is already accomplished. What we usually call salvation — justification — is the application by the Holy Spirit of that accomplished redemption to the individual who comes to Christ through faith. The redemption for the elect has already happened, but it is not applied in history until the person comes to faith in Jesus Christ. It is as if God established a trust and put everything needed for redemption in that trust — and through faith we draw from that trust.

## Clause 4: Effectual Calling

The paragraph says the elect "are effectually called unto faith in Christ by His Spirit working in due season." This doctrine of effectual calling is also called irresistible grace — the "I" in TULIP. It is also called regeneration or the new birth, and in a less technical sense, conversion.

The Shorter Catechism helps us here. Question 31 asks: "What is effectual calling?" Answer: "Effectual calling is the work of God's Spirit, whereby, convincing us of our sin and misery, enlightening our minds in the knowledge of Christ, and renewing our wills, He doth persuade and enable us to embrace Jesus Christ, freely offered to us in the gospel."

In other words, effectual calling is spiritual heart transplant — the work of God through which he changes our hearts. And notice that it precedes faith: it is this that enables us to embrace Jesus Christ. All of this is happening before we actually believe. It is preparing us for belief. That is why the new birth happens before faith. That is the whole point of Jesus's conversation with Nicodemus in John 3: unless you are born again, you cannot even understand your need for Christ. That has to happen first.

There is a general call that goes out to everyone who hears the gospel. Isaiah 55:1: "Ho! Everyone who thirsts, come to the waters." Jesus says, "Come to Me, all you who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest." The general call goes out to everyone. But the only people who respond to that call are those who have been elected by God — and not because there are two different calls. It is the same call going out to everyone, but only the elect respond to the preaching of God's Word.

Jesus says in John 6:44–45: "No one can come to Me unless the Father who sent Me draws him; and I will raise him up at the last day. It is written in the prophets, 'And they shall all be taught by God.' Therefore everyone who has heard and learned from the Father comes to Me."

Effectual calling is a work of God's free grace. It may not be instantaneous — God may be working with a person over a period of time, bringing them to the point where they trust in the Lord Jesus Christ.

The different names for this doctrine emphasize different aspects. Irresistible grace emphasizes that God will accomplish his work — if God wants to save a person, that person cannot permanently resist. It would be the height of arrogance to think that a creature could permanently frustrate the saving purposes of God. Regeneration emphasizes the extent of the work — it is not a small change, but a new birth, a new everything. Conversion emphasizes the viewpoint of the one going through it — a turning from sin and a turning to God.

## Clause 5: Justified, Adopted, and Sanctified

**Justification** means to declare righteous — treating a person as righteous regardless of their actual personal righteousness. In justification, nothing happens in you — there is no change in you. It is all happening outside of you. It is God's declaration. The Shorter Catechism, question 33: "What is justification? Justification is an act of God's free grace, wherein He pardoneth all our sins, and accepteth us as righteous in His sight, only for the righteousness of Christ imputed to us, and received by faith alone."

What do we contribute to our justification? Faith. Faith is the only thing we contribute. It is the instrument — not a meritorious work, not earning brownie points with God, but the instrument God uses to declare us righteous.

**Adoption** is often overlooked. The Westminster Confession is the only Reformed confession to dedicate an entire chapter to the doctrine of adoption (chapter 12). The Shorter Catechism, question 34: "What is adoption? Adoption is an act of God's free grace, whereby we are received into the number, and have a right to all the privileges, of the sons of God."

The moment you believe, you are a child of God — which means before that, you are not. Paul says in Romans 8:15: "For you did not receive the spirit of bondage again to fear, but you received the Spirit of adoption by whom we cry out, 'Abba, Father.'" And in Romans 8:23: we ourselves also eagerly wait for the adoption — the redemption of our bodies. We have been adopted, but we are still waiting for the final adoption, which is the resurrection.

Paul says in Ephesians 1:5 that God predestined us to adoption as sons by Jesus Christ to himself, according to the good pleasure of his will. Some translations put a footnote saying "sons" here means sons and daughters, or children. Paul could have written it that way if he wanted. He intentionally says "sons" because there are legal implications. In the first century, women could not be adopted legally in Roman law — they could not inherit their adopted father's name or possess the benefits of adoption. Not so with God. Men and women are fully children of God with all the benefits that come from being adopted by the God of heaven, the Creator of all things. That is how magnificent this doctrine is.

**Sanctification** is the working out of our faith in Christ. If you are not growing in sanctification, you have reason to question whether you have a genuine faith in Christ, because they go hand in hand. Sanctification is becoming more and more conformed to the image of Christ — working with God because God is working in us, as Philippians 2:12–13 teaches.

## Clause 6: "Kept by His Power Through Faith unto Salvation" — Perseverance of the Saints

The elect are "kept by His power through faith unto salvation" — here salvation is used in the ultimate sense where body and soul will be saved at the return of Christ.

This doctrine is also known as the perseverance of the saints — the "P" in TULIP. In order to assure that his elect will complete the journey, God took into his own hands the job of keeping them to the end. Jude 24–25:

> *"Now to Him who is able to keep you from stumbling, and to present you faultless before the presence of His glory with exceeding joy, to God our Savior, who alone is wise, be glory and majesty, dominion and power, both now and forever. Amen."* (Jude 24–25)

Note: the joy there is not ours — it is the joy of the One who presents us. That is what the Lord does for us.

Jesus says in John 6:37: "All that the Father gives Me will come to Me, and the one who comes to Me I will by no means cast out."

In Colossians 3, Paul says that we are in Christ, in the hand of Christ and in the Father — as if both the Son and the Father have their hands around us, and there is no way that anyone can pry us out.

## Clause 7: "Neither are any other... saved, but the Elect only" — Definite Atonement

Christ died for the elect. This doctrine is called limited atonement or particular atonement — the "L" in TULIP. Christ died for a particular people. Everything we have been talking about only works for those who were chosen by God before the foundation of the world — that is for whom Christ died.

Be assured: there is no one in hell for whom Christ died. It is not as if there is someone in hell that Christ really wanted to save but somehow was unable to. Everyone for whom Christ died will be saved.

## Paragraph 8: Three Purposes of Preaching Election

**First, the praise of God.** Every sermon of Jesus included teaching on election — the Sermon on the Mount, the Upper Room Discourse. The apostolic preaching in Acts 2, 3, 4, 13 and beyond also included it. Paul says in Ephesians 1:5–6 that God predestined us to adoption as sons, "to the praise of the glory of His grace." Why did he do all this? For praise. That was Paul's reaction after working through Romans 9–11. He ends with:

> *"Oh, the depth of the riches both of the wisdom and knowledge of God! How unsearchable are His judgments and His ways past finding out!"* (Romans 11:33)

And Jesus himself in Luke 10 says: "Nevertheless do not rejoice in this, that the spirits are subject to you, but rather rejoice because your names are written in heaven."

**Second, the comfort of God's people.** Paul says in Romans 8:33: "Who shall bring a charge against God's elect? It is God who justifies." No accusation stands against the one God has justified. Whatever sins could be brought against us — God knows all of them and far worse — and yet he has justified us in his Son. Because of that, he loves us, and it does not matter what is said against us: it has been covered by the blood of Christ.

**Third, humility.** It is not in us. We did not save ourselves. We did not cause ourselves to be who we are. It is all of God. Paul says in Romans 11:5–6: "Even so then, at this present time there is a remnant according to the election of grace. And if by grace, then it is no longer of works; otherwise grace is no longer grace." Therefore there is no boasting except in the cross of the Lord Jesus Christ. Paul says in 2 Corinthians: not that we are sufficient of ourselves, but our sufficiency is from God. It is the grace of God that equips us.

Humility flows from the doctrine of election. Everything you are as a believer — you are by the grace of God.
